text: The patient is positioned in dorsal recumbency, with the surgical area clipped widely for multiple trocar placements. Trochars are arranged in a semi-circle around the spleen, with the first trocar placed on the midline, caudal to the umbilicus. A modified Hassan technique is used to enter the abdomen, involving a skin incision and fascial stitches lateral to the linea alba. Hemostats are used to ensure safe abdominal entry before inserting the trocar and connecting insufflation tubing to achieve abdominal insufflation.

Additional ports are placed to triangulate visualization and instrument placement. The splenic mass is visible, and the surgeon uses the Asculap Cayman vessel sealing device to take down the falciform ligament, enhancing visualization of the surgical field.

The surgeon elevates the spleen using a blunt instrument to avoid penetrating the capsule. The vessel sealing device is applied near the hilus to detach the connecting ligaments. The procedure begins at the caudal aspect of the spleen, progressing cranially to perform a hilar splenectomy, ensuring complete vessel sealing near the hilus.

An instrument retracts tissues away from the vessel sealing device to prevent thermal spread to nearby intestines or organs. The assistant uses a blunt instrument to retract the spleen. A vessel that was incompletely sealed and cut is grasped and occluded by the surgeon's instrument. The vessel sealing device is then applied to seal the vessel completely without cutting the remaining tissue.

The surgeon continues the splenectomy towards the head of the spleen, following along the hilus on the caudal aspect near the short gastric vessels. The camera port is adjusted to enhance visualization of the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a sterile camera sleeve as a catch bag for the spleen, with a pre-placed purse string suture of two O nylon securing the sleeve. Instruments hold the mouth of the sleeve open as the spleen, along with its associated mass, is carefully manipulated into the bag. The spleen is inserted mass first to minimize the risk of abdominal contamination in case of splenic capsule rupture. Once fully inserted, the drawstring is pulled to close the bag securely.

The surgeon pulls the drawstring through the trocar and out of the abdomen, enlarging the incision to approximately 2.5 centimeters to partially exteriorize the bag. The spleen is then morcellated, removing it in large pieces to facilitate histopathologic diagnosis and reduce morbidity. Large pieces of the spleen are visibly extracted using this method.
